ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 refers to glutinous rice as a food item; the cooked form is the closest defensible match. No exact FoodOn URI for cooked white glutinous rice was identified.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC is used for glutinous rice in cooked form; this is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor available. Exact code for cooked white glutinous rice may vary by FoodEx2 version.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for cooked white glutinous rice as a food item in the clinical terminology system.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of cooked white glutinous rice is approximately 174g (about 1 cup), as commonly consumed in Singapore as part of a snack or meal accompaniment.

nutrient_source.title: Cooked white glutinous rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for cooked white glutinous rice (CR ID: F05090009). All per-100g values are taken from this canonical source. Saturated fat and fatty acid fractions not separately reported by HPB for this entry; values estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked glutinous rice (NDB 20444) as the closest approved secondary source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glutinous rice GI is reported in the range of 86–98 across multiple peer-reviewed studies (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care; Foster-Powell et al. 2002, Am J Clin Nutr). A representative GI of 92 is used, consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white glutinous rice (sticky rice). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (92) × carbohydrate per serving (21.09g × 1.74 serving factor = 36.7g) / 100 = 33.8. This is a high-GL food.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (5mg/100g), negligible sugar, no saturated fat
hypertension.guidance: Cooked white glutinous rice is very low in sodium (5mg/100g) and contains negligible saturated fat, making it acceptable for hypertension management; however, patients should pair it with potassium-rich foods to support DASH diet goals and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as coconut milk sauces or salted fillings.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (~92), near-complete amylopectin starch, very low dietary fibre (1.0g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cooked white glutinous rice has one of the highest glycemic indices of any grain (GI ~92) due to its near-complete amylopectin starch structure, causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit consumption, restrict portions to no more than 50g, and substitute with lower-GI whole grains such as brown rice, quinoa, or barley.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High glycemic load promotes triglyceride elevation; very low fibre; negligible saturated fat and sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While cooked white glutinous rice is very low in saturated fat (0.04g/100g) and sodium (5mg/100g), its very high glycemic load (GL ~34 per serving) and minimal dietary fibre (1.0g/100g) can promote postprandial triglyceride elevation and contribute to cardiometabolic risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion sizes and pair with fibre-rich vegetables and lean proteins.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High glycemic load promotes systemic inflammation; very low fibre; no anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cooked white glutinous rice is a highly refined, high-GI carbohydrate with only 1.0g dietary fibre per 100g and no positive anti-inflammatory factors (no omega-3, antioxidants, polyphenols, or whole-food fibre above 3g); individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this food, particularly during active flares, and substitute with anti-inflammatory whole grains.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cooked white glutinous rice (also known as sticky rice or sweet rice) is a staple grain food widely consumed across Southeast Asia, including Singapore, Malaysia, Thailand, and China. It is prepared by soaking raw white glutinous rice (Oryza sativa var. glutinosa) and then steaming or boiling until fully cooked, resulting in a translucent white, soft, and intensely sticky texture due to its near-complete amylopectin starch composition (very low amylose content). The flavour is mild and slightly sweet. Per 100g cooked, it provides approximately 97 kcal, 2.02g protein, 0.19g fat, and 21.09g carbohydrate, with very low dietary fibre (1.0g), minimal sodium (5mg), and negligible sugar (0.05g). Clinically, cooked white glutinous rice has one of the highest glycemic indices of any grain (GI ~92, high category), making it a high-glycemic-load food that causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation. It is contraindicated or strongly restricted for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and rheumatoid arthritis due to its pro-inflammatory glycemic profile and absence of anti-inflammatory nutrients. It is acceptable for hypertension due to its very low sodium content. In Singaporean and Southeast Asian culinary culture, glutinous rice is used in festive and everyday preparations including lo mai gai (lotus leaf glutinous rice), nasi pulut, tang yuan, and various kueh (traditional cakes). It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and kosher.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (canonical primary source for Singapore food records, CR ID F05090009, confidence 0.95). GI value of 92 is consistent with multiple peer-reviewed publications and the University of Sydney GI Database for white glutinous rice. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked glutinous rice (NDB 20444) as the closest approved secondary source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.04g/100g), monounsaturated fat (0.06g/100g), and polyunsaturated fat (0.05g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked glutinous rice (NDB 20444) as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this entry. Trans fat is reported as 0.0g — confirmed negligible per USDA source. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed micronutrient and moisture data for cooked white glutinous rice are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for CR ID F05090009, and USDA FoodData Central NDB 20444 does not provide complete coverage for all listed fields as of 2026: vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Both HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central (NDB 20444) were consulted and confirmed unavailability of these specific micronutrient values for this food entry. 3. Glycemic index of 92 is a representative value from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care; Foster-Powell et al. 2002, Am J Clin Nutr) for white glutinous rice; the GI range reported in literature is 86–98. 4. Cholesterol is 0.0mg — confirmed zero per HPB source; this is a plant-based grain food that naturally contains no cholesterol. 5. Glutinous rice is naturally gluten-free despite its name; the term 'glutinous' refers to its sticky texture, not gluten content. 6. The food is classified as a Singaporean/Southeast Asian staple; multilingual names are provided in Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per G6 mandatory language rules for Singaporean dishes.
